 {"id":"br-kpvhy","title":"Attention Feed Phase 2b: Ship --robot-events as the raw replay/feed surface","description":"# Why this bead exists
Robot clients need one raw, replayable, machine-oriented surface that answers "what happened since cursor N?" without forcing a full snapshot every time. `--robot-events` should be that surface.

# Scope
1. Add a canonical `--robot-events` command in robot mode.
2. Support cursor-based replay, filtering, and an obvious streaming/follow mode when appropriate.
3. Follow existing robot conventions for exit codes, `success`, `timestamp`, and structured error payloads.
4. Return cursor metadata in a way that makes cursor chaining straightforward for agent consumers.
5. Keep the interface simple and enumerable. Prefer explicit filters (`--session`, `--panes`, `--type`, `--category`, `--actionability`, `--since-cursor`, `--limit`) over a clever DSL.
6. Ensure the command's cursor/filter semantics line up exactly with the snapshot bootstrap path and later digest/wait/attention surfaces.
7. Make follow-mode behavior explicit about how quiet periods are represented, including whether the command stays silent, emits periodic liveness markers, or delegates liveness expectations entirely to the caller.

# Design considerations
- This command should be useful both for one-shot replay and long-lived operator loops.
- If a cursor is expired, the response must say so clearly and tell the caller to resync via snapshot.
- The command should not require a consumer to understand server internals or websocket topics.
- Quiet follow-mode behavior must be documented well enough that agents do not mistake a healthy idle stream for a broken one.

# Detailed verification expectations
- CLI golden tests for replay, filter, streaming/follow mode, quiet-period behavior, and expired-cursor responses.
- Tests for parity with the underlying normalized feed service.
- Transcript-style Go scenario coverage showing: snapshot -> events replay -> additional follow-mode wake.
- Go-based e2e harness coverage that captures stdout/stderr/log artifacts for raw event replay, quiet follow-mode, and expiration handling.
- Detailed failure logging that shows requested cursor, delivered cursor range, filter set, quiet-period behavior, and resync hint when assertions fail.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `--robot-events` exists as the raw replay/feed surface.
- [ ] Cursor chaining is obvious from the response shape.
- [ ] Structured filtering exists without introducing a bespoke query language.
- [ ] Error cases such as expired cursors or invalid filters are explicit and machine-actionable.
- [ ] Snapshot/bootstrap semantics and events replay semantics match exactly.
- [ ] Follow-mode quiet-period semantics are explicit enough that operators can distinguish healthy idleness from broken behavior.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":180,"created_at":"2026-03-21T00:49:20.262903165Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T08:36:11.722855032Z","closed_at":"2026-03-21T08:36:11.721759810Z","close_reason":"events.go implements --robot-events with cursor replay, filtering (session/pane/agent/category/actionability), hasMore detection, replay window metadata, cursor expiration handling with resync hints.  {"id":"br-la5zy","title":"Overlay-Feed T5: Integrate F12 overlay toggle with assign --watch mode","description":"# Why this bead exists
`assign --watch` is one of the most likely places a human operator will sit while ntm is making decisions continuously. If overlay access is awkward there, one of the most important operational workflows remains disconnected from the new attention surfaces. This bead makes the watch workflow discover and use the overlay in a way that is actually useful, not just cosmetically bound.

# Scope
1. Detect when `assign --watch` is running inside the monitored tmux session and provide an explicit, one-time overlay hint in that context.
2. Ensure overlay binding/setup is present when the workflow can genuinely use it.
3. Make the hint and workflow point toward the attention-aware overlay experience rather than a generic dashboard detour.
4. Keep the behavior quiet and context-sensitive so remote/non-tmux usage does not get confusing noise.
5. Leave the watch loop semantics themselves unchanged; this is discoverability and ergonomics, not an assignment-engine rewrite.

# Design considerations
- This workflow should not advertise attention integration before the overlay attention panel exists.
- The hint should feel like a useful affordance, not nagware.
- Any auto-setup must stay consistent with the existing overlay binding logic rather than inventing a second path.

# Detailed verification expectations
- Unit tests for environment detection and hint emission/non-emission.
- Tests for overlay binding setup behavior when the binding already exists versus when it must be installed.
- Transcript scenarios showing `assign --watch` inside and outside the target tmux session.
- Detailed logs in tests that print session context, tmux detection result, whether the hint was emitted, and whether binding setup ran.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] `assign --watch` surfaces overlay access only in the contexts where it is genuinely useful.
- [ ] The workflow points users toward the attention-aware overlay rather than a generic dashboard path.
- [ ] Binding setup remains aligned with existing overlay behavior.
- [ ] Automated tests and transcript artifacts make regressions easy to diagnose.","notes":"Implementation note: Part 1 (F12 hint + auto-setup) can be implemented immediately — it has no dependency on the Attention Feed and only touches existing overlay infrastructure. Parts 2 (event publishing) and 3 (watch progress badge) require the event envelope from Phase 1a. The br-xmmaj dep covers Parts 2-3. Implementers should ship Part 1 first as a quick win, then return for Parts 2-3 after the feed lands.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:08:17.964797323Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T08:14:10.316328786Z","closed_at":"2026-03-21T08:14:10.315182457Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["assign","attention-feed","cli","overlay","phase-1"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-la5zy","depends_on_id":"br-x66gc","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:10:39.291585247Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-la5zy","depends_on_id":"br-xmmaj","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:16:26.172242130Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-mamo3","title":"Attention Feed Phase 4b2: Build transcript-style e2e operator-loop scenarios and artifact logging","description":"# Why this bead exists
Transcript-style operator-loop scenarios are too important to leave implicit, and they now need to cover the human-facing summary surfaces as well as the raw robot loop. This bead exists to build the long-form scenario harnesses and artifact logging that prove the full user experience actually works end to end.

# Scope
1. Build Go-based e2e scenarios under `e2e/` for the canonical operator loop:
   - cold-start snapshot then cursor handoff
   - raw event replay after snapshot
   - wake on stalled pane
   - wake on ack-required mail
   - wake on reservation/file conflict
   - `--robot-attention` repeating cleanly across multiple wake cycles
   - resync behavior after expired cursor or process restart
2. Add transcript scenarios that explicitly compare low-noise operator mode versus high-fidelity debug mode on the same session state.
3. Add transcript scenarios for dashboard and terse output before and after meaningful attention changes so the human-facing frontier is validated too.
4. Ensure scenarios use the shared Go scenario/artifact harness instead of inventing separate fixture and logging conventions.
5. Ensure scenarios write detailed, stable artifacts to predictable locations under repo-controlled test artifact paths rather than ad hoc shell-script logs.
6. Make the Go harness verbose enough that a future maintainer can reconstruct what happened from the artifacts alone.
7. Keep scenarios honest about any partially supported or unsupported conditions, especially `bead_orphaned` if that remains unavailable.
8. Cover CLI and network-oriented paths where end-to-end user experience differs materially.
9. Use Go-based harness code as the primary test vehicle; do not rely on shell-script test runners as the canonical implementation.

# Logging / artifact expectations
- Transcript artifacts should record each command issued, key output excerpts, cursor values, wake reasons, active profiles, summary/view state, and resync transitions.
- Failures should leave behind step-by-step breadcrumbs rather than a single pass/fail line.
- Artifact locations should be explicit in the bead and in the eventual implementation/test output.
- The shared Go harness should support a retention/debug mode so artifacts can be kept when a test fails or when a maintainer explicitly asks for them.

# Acceptance criteria
- [ ] Go-based transcript-style e2e coverage exists for the canonical operator loop, its main failure/recovery paths, and the human-facing summary surfaces.
- [ ] The scenarios reuse the shared Go scenario/artifact harness rather than duplicating fixture plumbing.
- [ ] Operator-mode versus debug-mode behavior is exercised explicitly.
- [ ] Artifact logging is detailed enough to debug failures after the fact.
- [ ] The scenarios preserve all supported functionality and represent unsupported conditions honestly.
- [ ] The primary e2e harness lives in Go under the repo's normal test structure rather than shell scripts.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","estimated_minutes":180,"created_at":"2026-03-21T01:01:35.840054320Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-21T09:34:38.098404797Z","closed_at":"2026-03-21T09:34:38.097752165Z","close_reason":"Commit f63a1090: comprehensive e2e operator loop test coverage with transcript scenarios and artifact logging.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["attention-feed","e2e","phase-4","robot","transcripts","verification"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-mamo3","depends_on_id":"br-3qs46","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:01:45.249498041Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-mamo3","depends_on_id":"br-7746h","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:25:31.366462129Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-mamo3","depends_on_id":"br-91gti","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:11:29.975084804Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-mamo3","depends_on_id":"br-auag6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:11:30.263944519Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-mamo3","depends_on_id":"br-slg9g","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:01:44.053154331Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-mamo3","depends_on_id":"br-t540i","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:01:44.475968365Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-mamo3","depends_on_id":"br-tsqxo","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-21T01:01:44.873578633Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"br-obaln","title":"Phase 3a: Decompose dashboard.go (7017 LOC) into Logical Sub-Files","description":"# Decompose dashboard.go (7017 LOC) into Logical Sub-Files

## Background
7,017-line monolith — classic anti-pattern. All files stay in package dashboard.
ZERO LOGIC CHANGE — every function moves verbatim.

## Estimate: 120 minutes
This is a mechanical file-split. Each step is: cut code, paste to new file, add imports,
run go build. The Go compiler catches issues instantly. 7017 LOC ÷ ~800 LOC/file = ~9 files.
At ~12 min per file (cut, paste, fix imports, build) = ~108 min. Rounded to 120m.

## Target File Structure
  dashboard/
  ├── model.go         (~400)  Model struct, New(), constants, PostQuitAction
  ├── messages.go      (~200)  All custom message types
  ├── init_cmd.go      (~300)  Init(), initial fetch commands
  ├── update.go        (~1400) Update(), keyboard handling
  ├── update_data.go   (~1100) Data update handlers (accept*Msg)
  ├── view.go          (~800)  View(), header, footer
  ├── view_panels.go   (~1500) Content section, panel grid
  ├── helpers.go       (~800)  Formatting, utilities
  ├── run.go           (~220)  Run(), RunPopup(), RunWithOptions()

## AGENTS.md Compliance: No File Deletion
Per AGENTS.md RULE NUMBER 1: "NEVER delete a file without express permission."
After all code is extracted, dashboard.go should be reduced to a package-level comment:
  // Package dashboard — see model.go, update.go, view.go for implementation.
  // This file intentionally left as a redirect after decomposition.
  package dashboard
Do NOT delete dashboard.go without explicit user permission.

## CRITICAL: Zero Logic Changes
Every function moves verbatim. No renaming, restructuring, or improvements.
git diff should show only file additions and dashboard.go deletions.

## Implementation Steps
1. Create model.go → move Model struct, New(), constants → go build
2. Create messages.go → move all *Msg types → go build
3. Create init_cmd.go → move Init() → go build
4. Continue for each file (build after each move)
5. Reduce dashboard.go to redirect comment
6. go test -short ./internal/tui/dashboard/...

## Unit Tests (verify no regression)

### Pre/post test comparison protocol
  # Before:
  go test -v -count=1 ./internal/tui/dashboard/... > /tmp/dash_before.log 2>&1
  # After:
  go test -v -count=1 ./internal/tui/dashboard/... > /tmp/dash_after.log 2>&1
  # Compare (only timestamps should differ):
  diff <(grep '^---' /tmp/dash_before.log) <(grep '^---' /tmp/dash_after.log)

### New structural test: internal/tui/dashboard/structure_test.go
  func TestModelStructExists(t *testing.T) { var m Model; _ = m }
  func TestNewReturnsValidModel(t *testing.T) {
      m := New("test", "/tmp/test")
      if m.session != "test" { t.Error("session not set") }
  }
  func TestRunFunctionExists(t *testing.T) {
      var f func(string, string) (*PostQuitAction, error) = Run
      _ = f
  }

## E2E / VHS Verification
VHS captures MUST be pixel-identical to Phase 2 baselines.
Run all 5 profiles. Any visual difference = bug in the split.

## Logging
- No runtime logging changes (structural refactor only)
- Log decomposition completion: file count, LOC per file

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] dashboard.go reduced to redirect comment (not deleted)
- [ ] All code in logical sub-files (9 new files)
- [ ] go build ./cmd/ntm succeeds
- [ ] go vet ./internal/tui/dashboard/... clean
- [ ] All existing tests pass with identical results
- [ ] structure_test.go passes
- [ ] VHS captures pixel-identical to Phase 2
- [ ] No import cycles

## Files to Create
model.go, messages.go, init_cmd.go, update.go, update_data.go, view.go,
view_panels.go, helpers.go, run.go

Structural verification tests go in existing helpers_test.go — no new test file.
(AGENTS.md: no file proliferation)

 {"id":"br-ofwdn","title":"Phase 1c: Replace Custom Spinner with bubbles/spinner","description":"# Replace Custom Spinner with bubbles/spinner

## Background
internal/tui/components/spinner.go (130 LOC) defines a custom Spinner struct (NOT SpinnerModel)
with Init(), Update(), View(), and TickCmd() methods. It has frame sets, color, gradient support,
and respects AnimationsEnabled().

## ACTUAL Current API (verified from source)
  type Spinner struct {
      Style          SpinnerStyle
      Color          lipgloss.Color
      Frame          int
      FPS            time.Duration
      Label          string
      Gradient       bool
      GradientColors []string
  }
  func NewSpinner() Spinner
  func (s Spinner) Init() tea.Cmd
  func (s Spinner) Update(msg tea.Msg) (Spinner, tea.Cmd)
  func (s Spinner) View() string
  func (s Spinner) TickCmd() tea.Cmd

Used in: dashboard.go, layout.go, components_test.go
AnimationsEnabled() checked at: spinner.go lines 74, 84, 119

## What bubbles/spinner Provides
  type Model struct { Spinner Spinner; Style lipgloss.Style }
  func New(opts ...Option) Model
  func (m Model) Update(msg tea.Msg) (Model, tea.Cmd)
  func (m Model) View() string
  func (m Model) Tick() tea.Cmd  // returns TickMsg

## Implementation Plan — PRESERVE EXACT API SURFACE

### 1. Keep Spinner struct, change internals
The Spinner struct must keep its EXACT field names and method signatures because dashboard.go
and layout.go reference them directly. Internal implementation changes to delegate to
bubbles/spinner.

### 2. Key change: store bubbles spinner.Model internally
  type Spinner struct {
      inner          bspinner.Model  // bubbles/spinner
      Style          SpinnerStyle    // preserved field
      Color          lipgloss.Color  // preserved field
      Label          string          // preserved field
      Gradient       bool            // preserved field
      GradientColors []string        // preserved field
      // Frame and FPS no longer needed — bubbles handles internally
  }

### 3. Map SpinnerStyle to bubbles presets
  func mapStyle(s SpinnerStyle) bspinner.Spinner {
      switch s {
      case SpinnerDots: return bspinner.Dot
      case SpinnerLine: return bspinner.Line
      // ... etc
      }
  }

### 4. View() delegates to inner.View() with gradient overlay
  func (s Spinner) View() string {
      if !AnimationsEnabled() { return staticChar(s.Style) }
      base := s.inner.View()
      if s.Gradient { return applyGradient(base, s.GradientColors) }
      return base
  }

### 5. Update() delegates to inner.Update()
### 6. TickCmd() wraps inner.Tick()

## Unit Tests (REQUIRED)

### Expand: internal/tui/components/components_test.go (spinner section)
  func TestSpinnerAllStyles(t *testing.T) {
      // Verify NewSpinner() produces valid spinner for each style
      for _, style := range allSpinnerStyles() {
          s := NewSpinner()
          s.Style = style
          view := s.View()
          if view == "" { t.Errorf("style %d empty view", style) }
      }
  }

  func TestSpinnerReducedMotion(t *testing.T) {
      t.Setenv("NTM_ANIMATIONS", "0")
      s := NewSpinner()
      v1 := s.View()
      s, _ = s.Update(s.TickCmd())
      v2 := s.View()
      // Reduced motion: view should not change between ticks
  }

  func TestSpinnerGradient(t *testing.T) {
      s := NewSpinner()
      s.Gradient = true
      s.GradientColors = []string{"#ff0000", "#00ff00"}
      view := s.View()
      if view == "" { t.Error("gradient spinner empty") }
  }

  func TestSpinnerTickCmd(t *testing.T) {
      s := NewSpinner()
      cmd := s.TickCmd()
      if cmd == nil { t.Error("TickCmd should return non-nil") }
  }

  func BenchmarkSpinnerView(b *testing.B) {
      s := NewSpinner()
      b.ResetTimer()
      for i := 0; i < b.N; i++ { _ = s.View() }
  }

## E2E / VHS Verification
Spinners appear in dashboard loading states. Run ntm-dashboard-empty capture.

## Logging
- Debug log style mapping: "spinner: style %d -> bubbles %v"
- Debug log when AnimationsEnabled() returns false

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Spinner struct keeps exact same public API (fields + methods)
- [ ] All existing code in dashboard.go/layout.go compiles without changes
- [ ] All SpinnerStyle values map to valid bubbles presets
- [ ] Gradient overlay still works
- [ ] AnimationsEnabled() check preserved
- [ ] All existing spinner tests in components_test.go pass
- [ ] New tests pass
- [ ] VHS captures show correct spinner animation

## Files to Modify
- internal/tui/components/spinner.go (rewrite internals, keep API)

 {"id":"br-phdna","title":"EPIC: TUI Glamour Upgrade — Leverage Full Charmbracelet Ecosystem","description":"# TUI Glamour Upgrade Epic

## Executive Summary
NTM's TUI (49,213 LOC) is a sophisticated Bubble Tea application with 22 dashboard panels,
3 Catppuccin themes, responsive 5-tier layout, and custom animations. However, it dramatically
underutilizes the Charmbracelet ecosystem it depends on. The `harmonica` spring physics library
is in go.mod with ZERO imports. `bubbles/table`, `bubbles/viewport`, `bubbles/progress`,
`bubbles/spinner`, `bubbles/help`, and `bubbles/list` are all available but replaced by

 {"id":"br-pihrr","title":"Phase 2b: Add bubbles/table to History and Timeline Panels","description":"# Add bubbles/table to History and Timeline Panels

## Background
panels/history.go (862 LOC) renders tabular data with manual string formatting AND has
3 filter systems: historyStatusFilter (all/ok/error), historyAgentFilter (all/claude/codex/
gemini), historyTimeFilter (all/1h/24h/7d). It also has cursor management, replay on Enter,
and keybindings for filter cycling.

panels/timeline.go (1,111 LOC) similarly renders events with agent colors.

bubbles/table provides column alignment, scrolling, selection, and mouse support — but we
must PRESERVE the existing filter systems and replay functionality.

## CRITICAL: Existing Filter Architecture
  historyStatusFilter: Cycle with 'f' key → all/ok/error
  historyAgentFilter: Cycle with 'a' key → all/claude/codex/gemini
  historyTimeFilter: Cycle with 't' key → all/1h/24h/7d
  applyFilters(): Combines all 3 filters → produces filtered entry list
  moveCursor(): Manages selection within filtered results

bubbles/table does NOT have built-in filtering. The filter logic STAYS in the panel.
Only the RENDERING of filtered results moves to bubbles/table.

## Implementation Plan — History Panel

### 1. Add table.Model to HistoryPanel struct
  type HistoryPanel struct {
      PanelBase
      table    table.Model        // NEW: replaces inline rendering
      entries  []history.Entry    // raw data
      filtered []history.Entry    // after filters applied
      // ... existing filter fields preserved
  }

### 2. Define columns (adaptive to tier)
  func historyColumns(width int) []table.Column {
      if width < 120 { return narrowColumns() }
      if width < 200 { return splitColumns() }
      return wideColumns()
  }

### 3. Convert filtered entries → table.Row on each filter change
  func (m *HistoryPanel) rebuildTableRows() {
      rows := make([]table.Row, len(m.filtered))
      for i, e := range m.filtered {
          rows[i] = table.Row{formatTime(e.Timestamp), e.Command, e.Target, statusBadge(e.Status)}
      }
      m.table.SetRows(rows)
  }

### 4. Filter cycling preserved — after filter change, call rebuildTableRows()
### 5. Replay on Enter — get selected row index, map back to filtered[index]
### 6. Style with Catppuccin theme
### 7. Route table.Update(msg) when panel focused

## Timeline Panel — Similar approach
  Columns: Time, Event Type, Agent, Description
  Agent column uses AgentBadge for color coding
  No filter system (simpler than history)

 {"id":"br-xz14y","title":"Phase 1d: Replace Custom Progress Bar with bubbles/progress","description":"# Replace Custom Progress Bar with bubbles/progress

## Background — TWO SEPARATE PROGRESS BAR SYSTEMS EXIST

### System 1: styles package (styles/styles.go)
  - styles.ProgressBar(percent, width, filled, empty, colors...) string — line 340
  - styles.ShimmerProgressBar(percent, width, filled, empty, tick, colors...) string — line 366
  Used by: dashboard.go:4313, layout.go:241, panels/quota.go:199, panels/accounts.go:295,301,
           panels/context.go:361

### System 2: components package (components/progress.go)
  - components.ProgressBar struct with Width, Percent, ShowPercent, ShowLabel, GradientColors, etc.
  - components.NewProgressBar(width) *ProgressBar
  - components.IndeterminateBar struct for unknown-duration progress
  Used by: synthesizer/modes_view.go:344, synthesis_progress.go:75, panels/ensemble.go:403

### NO GradientProgressBar function exists (previous bead was wrong)

## Implementation Plan — Hybrid (preserve both systems)

### For System 1 (styles.ProgressBar):
These are stateless render functions. Replace the simple 2-color case with bubbles/progress:
  func ProgressBar(percent float64, width int, filled, empty string, colors ...string) string {
      if len(colors) <= 2 {
          // Use bubbles/progress
          var p progress.Model
          if len(colors) == 2 {
              p = progress.New(progress.WithGradient(colors[0], colors[1]))
          } else {
              p = progress.New(progress.WithDefaultGradient())
          }
          p.Width = width
          p.ShowPercentage = false
          return p.ViewAs(percent)
      }
      // Keep custom implementation for 3+ color gradients
      return customGradientBar(percent, width, filled, empty, colors)
  }

ShimmerProgressBar stays as-is (bubbles doesn't support shimmer animation).

### For System 2 (components.ProgressBar struct):
Keep the struct API unchanged. Internally, when Animated is false and GradientColors has <=2
entries, delegate to bubbles/progress for rendering. Otherwise use custom.

      targets  []FocusTarget

  }
  func (fr *FocusRing) Next() / Prev() — skip hidden targets
  func (fr *FocusRing) Current() FocusTarget
  func (fr *FocusRing) SetByID(id string) bool
  func (fr *FocusRing) Rebuild(targets []FocusTarget) — preserve focus if possible

  func TestFocusRingNext(t *testing.T) {
      fr := NewFocusRing([]FocusTarget{
          {ID: "a", Visible: func() bool { return true }},
          {ID: "b", Visible: func() bool { return true }},
          {ID: "c", Visible: func() bool { return true }},

      if fr.Current().ID != "a" { t.Error("start at first") }
      fr.Next()
      if fr.Current().ID != "b" { t.Error("next to b") }

      if fr.Current().ID != "a" { t.Error("wrap to a") }

  func TestFocusRingSkipsHidden(t *testing.T) {
      fr := NewFocusRing([]FocusTarget{
          {ID: "a", Visible: func() bool { return true }},
          {ID: "b", Visible: func() bool { return false }},

      })

  func TestFocusRingPrevWraps(t *testing.T) {

  func TestFocusRingSetByID(t *testing.T) {

  func TestFocusRingRebuildPreservesFocus(t *testing.T) {

          {ID: "c", Visible: func() bool { return true }},
      })
      if fr.Current().ID != "b" { t.Error("should preserve focus on b") }

  func TestFocusRingRebuildCurrentRemoved(t *testing.T) {

  func TestFocusRingNext(t *testing.T) {

      if fr.Current().ID != "b" { t.Error("next to b") }

 {"id":"ntm-00u","title":"Implement rate limit backoff for agents","description":"Detect rate limit errors and notify users (do not auto-retry).

## What This Task Does

Detect when agents hit rate limits and notify users. This is simpler
and safer than auto-retry, which can cause more problems than it solves.

## Why Detection Only (No Auto-Retry)

Auto-retry is complex and error-prone:
- Can't reliably capture the 'last command' to replay
- Agent state may be corrupted after rate limit
- Infinite retry loops are possible
- Users should decide if/when to retry

Instead: Detect, notify, let user handle it.

## Detection

Rate limit patterns in agent output:
- 'rate limit exceeded'
- 'too many requests'
- '429'
- 'quota exceeded'
- 'try again in X seconds'

## Response

When rate limit detected:
1. Mark agent as 'rate_limited' in health status
2. Fire notification event (desktop, webhook, shell)
3. Include wait time if parseable from error
4. Show in 'ntm health' output with clear indicator

Example notification:
'Agent cc_1 hit rate limit. Suggested wait: 60s'

## Integration

- Uses health check infrastructure (ntm-239) for detection
- Uses notification system (ntm-gcv) for alerts
- Shows in 'ntm status' with rate_limited indicator

## Configuration

[resilience.rate_limit]
detect = true
notify = true
patterns = ['rate limit', '429', 'too many requests']

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Rate limit patterns detected in pane output
- [ ] Notification fired when rate limit detected
- [ ] Wait time parsed when available
- [ ] Status shown in ntm health output
- [ ] No auto-retry (by design)

 {"id":"ntm-014q","title":"Feature: Policy Engine","description":"# Policy Engine (internal/policy/)

## Why This Is Critical
The .ntm/policy.yaml file is referenced by MULTIPLE features but there's no dedicated engine to parse and enforce it:
- Destructive Command Protection (blocked/allowed patterns)
- BD Daemon Mode (auto-push policy gate)
- File Reservation Lifecycle (force-release approval)
- SLB Safety Gates (approval_required operations)

Without a unified Policy Engine, each feature reimplements policy checking, leading to inconsistencies and bugs.

## Policy File Structure
```yaml
# .ntm/policy.yaml
version: 1

# Commands that are always blocked
blocked:
  - pattern: "git reset --hard"
    reason: "Hard reset loses commits"
  - pattern: "git checkout --"
    reason: "Discards uncommitted changes"

# Commands that require approval
approval_required:
  - pattern: "git push --force"
    reason: "Force push rewrites history"
    slb: true  # Requires SLB two-person rule
  - pattern: "force_release"
    reason: "Releases another agent's reservation"
    slb: false  # Just needs any approval

# Commands that bypass checks
allowed:
  - pattern: "git checkout -b"
    reason: "Create branch is safe"

# Automation settings
automation:
  auto_push: false
  auto_commit: true
  force_release: approval
```

## Implementation (internal/policy/engine.go)

### Policy Engine Interface
```go
type PolicyEngine struct {
    policyPath string
    policy     *Policy
    mu         sync.RWMutex
}

type Policy struct {
    Version          int              `yaml:"version"`
    Blocked          []PatternRule    `yaml:"blocked"`
    ApprovalRequired []PatternRule    `yaml:"approval_required"`
    Allowed          []PatternRule    `yaml:"allowed"`
    Automation       AutomationConfig `yaml:"automation"`
}

type PatternRule struct {
    Pattern    string   `yaml:"pattern"`
    Reason     string   `yaml:"reason"`
    SLB        bool     `yaml:"slb,omitempty"`
    Exceptions []string `yaml:"exceptions,omitempty"`
}
```

### Key Methods
```go
func (e *PolicyEngine) Load(path string) error
func (e *PolicyEngine) Reload() error  // Watch for file changes
func (e *PolicyEngine) IsBlocked(command string) (bool, string)
func (e *PolicyEngine) NeedsApproval(action string) (bool, *PatternRule)
func (e *PolicyEngine) IsAllowed(command string) bool
func (e *PolicyEngine) AutomationEnabled(feature string) bool
```

### File Watching
Policy file is watched for changes and automatically reloaded:
```go
func (e *PolicyEngine) StartWatcher(ctx context.Context) {
    watcher, _ := fsnotify.NewWatcher()
    watcher.Add(e.policyPath)
    // Reload on change
}
```

### Default Policy
If .ntm/policy.yaml doesn't exist, use sensible defaults (block dangerous patterns, require approval for force operations).

## Integration Points
- Destructive Command Protection: Uses IsBlocked(), IsAllowed()
- BD Daemon: Uses AutomationEnabled("auto_push")
- File Reservation: Uses NeedsApproval("force_release")
- SLB: Uses NeedsApproval() with SLB flag check

## New Commands
```bash
ntm policy show           # Display current policy
ntm policy validate       # Check policy file syntax
ntm policy reset          # Reset to defaults
ntm policy edit           # Open in editor
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Policy file parsed correctly
- [ ] Default policy used when file missing
- [ ] File watching with auto-reload
- [ ] IsBlocked/NeedsApproval/IsAllowed work
- [ ] AutomationEnabled checks work
- [ ] Validation command catches syntax errors","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-04T01:27:17.437246Z","updated_at":"2026-01-04T04:15:59.329965Z","closed_at":"2026-01-04T04:15:59.329965Z","close_reason":"Policy engine complete with: Policy struct (Version, Blocked, ApprovalRequired, Allowed, Automation), AutomationConfig (auto_commit, auto_push, force_release), SLB support, CLI commands (show, validate, reset, edit, automation), comprehensive tests.  {"id":"ntm-03ae","title":"Integrate routing with Agent Mail file reservations","description":"When routing work, consider Agent Mail file reservations as a signal for task affinity.

   - Cache results (refresh every 30s or on routing request)
   - Map: pane_id -> []reserved_paths

2. AFFINITY SCORING:
   - Extract file paths from prompt (patterns like "src/auth.go", "internal/")
   - Check if any agent has matching reservations
   - Add affinity bonus (configurable, default 30) to matching agent

3. CONFLICT PREVENTION:
   - If prompt mentions file reserved by another agent:
     - Warning in response: "File reserved by {agent}"
     - Option: --respect-reservations to skip reserved files

```toml
[routing.agent_mail]
enabled = true
reservation_bonus = 30
respect_reservations = false  # warn only vs enforce
cache_ttl = "30s"
```

- Requires Agent Mail MCP to be available
- Graceful degradation if unavailable
